question: What is the result of assigning one object to another in C++?
options: The member-wise copy of data members occurs
The objects become aliases to the same memory
The assignment operator must be explicitly defined
It results in a compiler error by default
explanation: In C++, object assignment performs a member-wise copy (shallow copy) of the data members from the source object to the destination object using the compiler-generated assignment operator.

question: When is it necessary to overload the assignment operator for a class?
options: Always, for every class
Only when the class contains constant members
When the class contains pointer members or resources that require deep copy
Only when the class is derived from another class
explanation: The assignment operator must be overloaded when a class manages resources (like dynamic memory) to ensure a proper deep copy and prevent issues like memory leaks or double deletion.

question: Which of the following correctly declares the overloaded assignment operator for a class 'MyClass'?
options: MyClass operator=(MyClass rhs);
void operator=(const MyClass& rhs);
MyClass& operator=(const MyClass& rhs);
MyClass operator=(const MyClass rhs);
explanation: The canonical form returns a reference to the invoking object (MyClass&) to allow chaining (a = b = c). It takes a const reference to the right-hand side object to avoid unnecessary copying and prevent modification of the source.

question: What is the 'self-assignment' check used for in an overloaded assignment operator?
options: To improve performance by skipping unnecessary work
To prevent a runtime error if an object is assigned to itself
It is a mandatory requirement for all assignment operators
To ensure the compiler optimizes the code
explanation: The self-assignment check (if(this == &rhs) return *this;) is crucial to prevent the operator from releasing resources and then trying to copy from the same now-invalidated source, which would lead to undefined behavior.

question: Considering the Rule of Three, if a class defines a destructor and a copy constructor, what else should it typically define?
options: A default constructor
A parameterized constructor
An overloaded assignment operator
A move constructor
explanation: The Rule of Three states that if a class requires a user-defined destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment operator, it likely requires all three. This is because all three are typically needed to manage the same resource correctly.
